remember: Recall the definitions of relations, types of relations (reflexive, symmetric, transitive, and equivalence relations)
Identify and describe examples of different types of relations
List and explain the properties of functions (one-to-one, onto, and into functions)
Recall the composition of functions and its properties
understand: Understand the concept of empty relation and universal relation
Explain the difference between a relation and a function
Determine whether a given relation is a function and justify the answer
Understand the concept of one-to-one correspondence and its relation to bijections
apply: Determine whether a given relation is reflexive, symmetric, or transitive
Determine whether a given function is one-to-one, onto, or a bijection
Find the domain, range, and co-domain of a given function
Compose two functions and determine the properties of the resulting function
analyze: Analyze and describe the properties of the composition of functions
Analyze and determine whether a given relation is an equivalence relation
Analyze and compare different types of relations and functions
Analyze and describe the difference between a relation and a function

ncert_objectives: Define a relation from a set to another set.
Distinguish between a relation and a function.
Give an example of a function which is one-one, onto and bijective.
Let A = {1,2,3} and B = {a,b,c}. Define a relation R from A to B such that R = {(1,a),(2,b),(3,c)}.
board_exam_objectives: Define a relation, function, one-to-one function, onto function and bijective function.
Determine whether a given relation is reflexive, symmetric, transitive or an equivalence relation.
Determine whether a given function is one-to-one, onto or a bijection.
Find the domain, range and co-domain of a given function, and determine whether two functions are equal or not.
